content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, backend developers have worked around the same limitation in HTTP.
When we need to retrieve data, the natural choice is GET. It is safe, idempotent, and well understood by browsers, proxies, and caching systems.
But what happens when the request becomes complex?
Imagine building a search API that supports dozens of filters, sorting options, pagination, nested conditions, and date ranges. Encoding all of this into a URL quickly becomes difficult to manage. URL length limitations also become a concern.
Most developers solve this by using POST.
Although it works technically, it introduces a semantic problem. POST is intended for requests that may change server state. Using it simply to retrieve data never felt like the right fit.
After years of discussions within the HTTP community, there is finally a standardized solution.
Introducing the QUERY Method
The HTTP QUERY method was standardized in RFC 10008 to support read operations that require a request body.
In simple terms, it combines the strengths of both GET and POST.
It allows clients to send complex request data in the body while preserving the semantics of a read only operation.
Why Do We Need It?
Let’s compare the existing approaches.
Using GET
GET /products?category=laptop&brand=Dell&sort=price&page=1&size=20This works well for simple requests.
As more filters are added, the URL becomes increasingly difficult to read, maintain, and sometimes exceeds practical length limits.
Using POST
Many APIs switch to something like this:
POST /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "laptop",
"brand": "Dell",
"sort": "price",
"page": 1,
"size": 20
}This solves the payload problem, but the HTTP method no longer accurately communicates the intent.
The operation is still reading data, yet POST suggests that something might be created or modified.
Using QUERY
With the new method, the same request becomes:
QUERY /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"category": "laptop",
"brand": "Dell",
"sort": "price",
"page": 1,
"size": 20
}Now the HTTP method correctly expresses the intent.
The server receives a request body, but the operation remains read only.
Benefits of QUERY
The new method offers several advantages.

• It is designed for read only operations.

• It supports a request body.

• It is idempotent.

• It can participate in caching when supported.

• It makes API intent much clearer.
For API consumers, this improves readability and makes the contract easier to understand.
Where Could QUERY Be Useful?
There are many scenarios where QUERY fits naturally.
Search APIs
Applications with advanced filtering and sorting often struggle with large query strings.
Reporting
Analytics dashboards frequently send complex filter objects that are difficult to represent in a URL.
Data Exploration
Business intelligence tools usually generate structured search requests that fit naturally into a request body.
Enterprise Applications
Large scale systems often expose search endpoints with many optional criteria. QUERY allows these APIs to express their intent without relying on POST.
Will We Start Using It Today?
Probably not.
Although QUERY is now part of the HTTP standard, adoption across the ecosystem will take time.
Frameworks, API gateways, reverse proxies, browsers, load balancers, SDKs, and developer tools all need to add support before it becomes a common choice.
In the meantime, many teams will continue using POST for complex read operations because it is widely supported.
That does not make QUERY any less valuable. Every standard needs time before it becomes part of everyday development.
What Does This Mean for REST APIs?
One of the biggest advantages of REST is that HTTP methods communicate intent.
GET retrieves data.
POST creates resources.
PUT replaces resources.
PATCH updates resources.
DELETE removes resources.
Until now, there was no standard method for performing a read operation with a request body.
QUERY fills that gap.
It allows developers to build APIs that are both expressive and semantically correct.
Final Thoughts
The introduction of QUERY is not a revolutionary change, but it is a meaningful one.
It addresses a problem that backend developers have encountered for years and provides a cleaner, standards based solution.
Whether QUERY becomes as common as GET or POST remains to be seen, but it is encouraging to see HTTP continue to evolve after decades of widespread use.
As engineers, understanding these changes helps us design APIs that are clearer, more consistent, and aligned with web standards.
Have you ever designed a search endpoint where GET felt too restrictive and POST felt semantically incorrect? If so, would you consider adopting QUERY once your framework and infrastructure support it?
I would love to hear your thoughts and experiences.
